prompt: Which object does the SAP kernel implicitly check every time a user starts a transaction?
explanation: S_TCODE is checked automatically by the kernel at transaction start, independent of any developer-written AUTHORITY-CHECK.

prompt: A composite role can hold its own authorization values independent of the roles it bundles.
explanation: Composite roles never carry authorization data - they only bundle single/derived roles for assignment convenience.

prompt: Match each SU24 check indicator to its effect.
dragTargets: Enforced but silently not proposed (no entry)

correctMapping.Unmaintained: Enforced but silently not proposed (no entry)

explanation: Each check indicator produces a distinct combination of enforcement and PFCG proposal behavior.

prompt: A master role's menu was changed last week. Nine of twelve derived roles work; three don't. What's the most likely cause?

options.text: The three derived roles were never regenerated after the master change
The three affected users have corrupted authorization buffers
The master role's SU25 step was skipped

explanation: Partial-subset failures after a master role change are the classic signature of derived roles not being included in the regeneration/mass-generation run.

prompt: Which factor should be decided FIRST when designing an enterprise role architecture for a 40-company-code landscape?

options.text: Which fields are treated as organizational-level
The exact PFCG icon color scheme
Whether to use SU53 or STAUTHTRACE for troubleshooting

explanation: Org-level field decisions determine whether the landscape gets clean, scalable derivation trees or an unmanageable explosion of single roles - it must be decided centrally and early.

prompt: Which user type should NEVER be permitted interactive dialog logon?
explanation: System users are meant purely for RFC/background communication; permitting interactive logon defeats individual accountability controls.

prompt: Locking a user in SU01 immediately terminates any of their currently active sessions.
explanation: Locking only prevents new logons; an already-active session continues until it naturally ends unless explicitly terminated via SM04/AL08.

prompt: A terminated employee's SAP account remained active for 25 days. What is the most robust long-term fix?

options.text: A daily reconciliation job comparing HR active employees against SAP active users, independent of the primary provisioning integration
Ask managers to email security when someone leaves
Increase the HR-to-SAP sync frequency to twice weekly

explanation: A reconciliation safety net independent of the primary integration catches failures the primary process itself can't detect.

prompt: What is the biggest audit risk of using a Reference User pattern broadly?

options.text: It's slower for users to log on
Standard role-assignment audit queries won't reveal the inherited authorizations unless specifically checked
It requires a password reset every 30 days

explanation: Reference user authorizations are an audit blind spot - they don't show up in standard 'who has role X' queries against the acting user.

prompt: Parameter IDs (SPA/GPA) restrict which values a user is authorized to enter.
explanation: Parameter IDs only pre-fill default values for convenience; they have zero bearing on actual authorization enforcement.

prompt: What enforces row-level security on an S/4HANA CDS view?
explanation: DCL (Data Control Language) roles define row-level restrictions on CDS views, enforced independently of classic AUTHORITY-CHECK.

prompt: A user who passes every classic authorization check can still see zero rows in a Fiori analytics app.
explanation: A DCL role restriction at the CDS view layer can filter all rows even when classic authorization fully passes - the two layers are independent.

prompt: A Fiori app shows different row counts in QA vs Production for the same user. What should you check first?

options.text: Whether the DCL role was transported alongside the backend PFCG role

explanation: DCL and PFCG roles are tracked by different transport object types and teams - a common cutover gap is DCL roles not being transported alongside their backend counterparts.

prompt: Match each Fiori security symptom to its most likely cause.
dragItems: Tile visible, app errors on open
dragTargets: Backend authorization or SICF service inactive

correctMapping.Tile visible, app errors on open: Backend authorization or SICF service inactive

explanation: Each symptom maps to a distinct layer of the Fiori security stack.

prompt: Assigning a Fiori catalog to a role automatically grants the backend data access the app needs.
explanation: Catalog assignment only controls tile visibility; backend OData authorization is a separate, independently required grant.

prompt: What must be true before an OData service is reachable at all, regardless of authorization?

options.text: Its SICF node must be activated
SU25 must have been run that day
The service must be in a composite role

explanation: SICF activation is a prerequisite gate before any authorization object is even evaluated for an OData call.

prompt: In a $batch request, if one sub-operation fails, all other sub-operations in the same batch also fail.
explanation: Each sub-operation in a $batch request is independently authorization-checked and can succeed or fail independently, which is exactly why partial-failure responses can be confusing to parse.

prompt: Which tool shows only the single most recent failed authorization check?
explanation: SU53's core limitation is capturing only the last failure, unlike full trace tools.

prompt: Match each tool to what it actually shows.
dragTargets: Every check over a trace window

correctMapping.STAUTHTRACE: Every check over a trace window

explanation: Each tool serves a fundamentally different diagnostic purpose.

prompt: A trace showing zero entries for an expected authorization object always means the check passed.
explanation: A missing trace entry means the check never executed at all (a different code path ran), not that it passed - a critical distinction.

prompt: How should authorization incidents ideally be detected in a mature landscape?

options.text: Proactive monitoring of security audit log failure-rate spikes correlated with recent transports

explanation: Proactive monitoring shifts detection from reactive ticket accumulation to catching regressions within minutes of the causing change.

prompt: What is the finest-grained BTP permission unit, defined in xs-security.json?
explanation: Scopes are the atomic permission unit; role templates group scopes, roles instantiate templates, and role collections bundle roles for assignment.

prompt: Removing a role collection from a user immediately invalidates any JWT already issued to them.
explanation: Already-issued JWTs remain valid until their natural expiry regardless of subsequent role collection changes - a key incident-response consideration.

prompt: Match each BTP authorization layer to its ABAP-world analogy.
explanation: The four BTP layers map conceptually, though not mechanically, onto the classic ABAP authorization hierarchy.

prompt: What does IAS Conditional Authentication primarily enable?

options.text: Risk-based step-up authentication (e.g., MFA) based on signals like location or app sensitivity

explanation: Conditional Authentication is IAS's risk-based rule engine, a core Zero Trust building block.

prompt: IAS always stores the actual corporate password when brokering federated authentication.
explanation: In broker/federated mode, IAS never sees or stores the corporate password - it only relays the authentication assertion.

prompt: SSO fails only for one specific business unit's users. What should you correlate first?

options.text: IAS trace logs together with the corporate IdP's federation logs for that business unit

explanation: Subset-specific SSO failures often trace to attribute-format differences on the corporate IdP side, requiring correlation across both federation legs.

prompt: What protocol does IPS typically use to communicate with cloud targets?
explanation: SCIM (System for Cross-domain Identity Management) is the standard protocol IPS uses for Create/Update/Deactivate operations on most cloud targets.

prompt: A provisioning filter should default to including users when a condition can't be evaluated.
explanation: Filters should fail closed (exclude by default on ambiguous conditions) to avoid unintended over-provisioning.

prompt: 200 users in a newly created cost center were never provisioned into a BTP app. What's the likely cause?

options.text: An allowlist-based filter that was never updated for the new cost center

explanation: Manually maintained allowlist filters silently exclude anything not explicitly listed, including new org units created after the filter was written.

prompt: What primarily causes a valid, non-expired leaf certificate to still fail trust validation?

options.text: An expired intermediate or root certificate in the chain

explanation: Certificate chain validation requires every level, not just the leaf, to be valid and present in the trusting system's PSE.

prompt: Kerberos SSO can fail due to clock skew between client, KDC, and server.
explanation: Kerberos tickets are time-sensitive; more than a few minutes of clock skew commonly breaks authentication.

prompt: Match each protocol to what it primarily provides.
dragTargets: Authentication + attribute exchange for web SSO
Ticket-based authentication in a trusted network domain

correctMapping.SAML2: Authentication + attribute exchange for web SSO
correctMapping.Kerberos: Ticket-based authentication in a trusted network domain

explanation: Each protocol solves a distinct problem despite frequent conflation in interviews.

prompt: Which OAuth grant type is used for Principal Propagation to an on-premise backend?
explanation: The SAML Bearer Assertion flow lets a cloud app present a SAML assertion vouching for the user's identity to obtain a backend-scoped token.

prompt: Cloud Connector allows inbound firewall openings to be avoided since the tunnel is always initiated outbound from on-premise.
explanation: Cloud Connector always initiates the tunnel outbound from the customer's network, avoiding inbound firewall rule changes.

prompt: A request fails before ever reaching the backend. Where should you look first?

options.text: Cloud Connector system/resource access control list

explanation: A rejection before reaching the backend at all points to Cloud Connector's own ACL layer, not backend authorization.

prompt: What HANA control restricts which rows of a calculation view a user can see?
explanation: Analytic Privileges enforce row-level restriction, distinct from column-level masking or object-level access grants.

prompt: A clean ABAP-layer authorization audit guarantees that data accessed via a direct SQL/BI tool connection is also protected.
explanation: Direct HANA access bypasses the ABAP layer entirely and requires its own independently designed privilege model.

prompt: Match each HANA control to its enforcement level.
explanation: Each HANA-native control operates at a distinct granularity.

prompt: What are secinfo/reginfo files, managed via SMGW, primarily responsible for?

options.text: Controlling which external programs/hosts may register or call RFC function modules at the gateway

explanation: Gateway ACLs (secinfo/reginfo) are evaluated before any application-layer authorization check even occurs.

prompt: Web Dispatcher and SAProuter protect the exact same protocol, so configuring one makes the other redundant.
explanation: Web Dispatcher handles HTTP(S); SAProuter handles classic RFC/DIAG protocol traffic - neither substitutes for the other.

prompt: A security assessment finds an empty secinfo file on a production system. What is the correct remediation approach?

options.text: Roll out a restrictive ACL immediately in production with no testing
Stage and test restrictive ACL configuration in non-production first, then roll out carefully
Ignore it since PFCG roles are already well designed
Only fix it during the next major upgrade

explanation: Overly aggressive ACL changes can break legitimate interfaces, so careful staged testing is essential before production rollout.

prompt: What is the difference between remediation and mitigation of an SoD risk?

options.text: Remediation removes the conflicting access; mitigation documents a compensating control when removal isn't feasible
Mitigation always requires firing the employee
Remediation only applies to firefighter access

explanation: Mitigation is the formal, auditable alternative when full remediation isn't organizationally feasible.

prompt: A clean GRC risk analysis report always reflects the current, accurate state of the landscape.
explanation: Risk analysis is only as current as the last successful connector sync - a silently failing sync can produce a stale, falsely-clean report.

prompt: Match each GRC process to whether it's preventive or detective.
dragTargets: Detective - periodic review of existing access

correctMapping.User Access Review (UAR): Detective - periodic review of existing access

explanation: A mature governance model requires both layers; relying on only one leaves a gap.

prompt: An SoD conflict can't be remediated due to a small team size. What should you do?

options.text: Formally document a mitigating control with a re-certification cadence
Informally acknowledge it verbally to the audit team
Ignore it since it's a known limitation
Grant SAP_ALL to simplify the analysis

explanation: An informal acknowledgment provides no audit evidence; a formally documented, re-certified mitigating control is the correct governance response.

prompt: What is the first step in a disciplined authorization incident RCA methodology?

options.text: Reproduce and classify the failure precisely

explanation: Precise reproduction and classification is the foundation every subsequent RCA step depends on.

prompt: SU25 is an automatic step that requires no manual review during an upgrade.
explanation: SU25 requires active review and merge decisions - skipping or rubber-stamping it is a leading cause of post-upgrade authorization surprises.

prompt: Under war-room pressure, stakeholders demand an immediate broad access grant. What's the better alternative?

options.text: A time-boxed Firefighter/emergency access grant with mandatory logging and review
A permanent role change to avoid future tickets
Ignoring the request until root cause is found

explanation: Time-boxed emergency access satisfies urgency while preserving governance and automatic expiry, unlike a permanent broad grant.

prompt: A production authorization incident should always be resolved by the security team alone.
explanation: Effective RCA often requires security, Basis, and functional teams together, since root causes can live in any of those layers.

prompt: What's the best architecture for identity governance across 5 pre-consolidation legacy ECC systems migrating to one S/4HANA instance?

options.text: A central IdM/IGA layer provisioning consistently across all 5 today, so only the target changes at consolidation
Five completely separate identity processes, one per system
Wait until consolidation to design any identity governance
Manual SU01 maintenance across all 5 systems

explanation: A unifying central layer avoids a second disruptive identity re-architecture at consolidation time.

prompt: A junior consultant keeps resolving tickets by setting objects to 'No Check'. What's the best leadership response?

options.text: Coach on the risk and add a mandatory review gate for SU24 check indicator changes
Ignore it since tickets are getting resolved quickly
Grant them PFCG production access to fix it themselves faster

explanation: This requires both individual coaching and a structural process safeguard, not just an individual correction.

prompt: CSRF token errors are technically a web-security control failure, not an authorization failure, even though they can look identical to one.
explanation: CSRF tokens are an anti-cross-site-request-forgery control, distinct from AUTHORITY-CHECK-based authorization enforcement.

prompt: An integration's batch requests started failing right after an OData entity was removed in a recent transport. What governance gap does this reveal?

options.text: OData entity deprecation wasn't treated as a breaking-change event requiring the same approval as any other API contract change
The integration used the wrong protocol

explanation: Deprecating an OData entity is a breaking API change and should go through the same governance as any other contract change.

prompt: When should an enterprise use multiple IAS tenants instead of one?

options.text: Only when genuine regulatory, M&A, or data residency requirements demand isolation configuration alone can't achieve

explanation: Single-tenant is the default; fragmentation should be justified by genuine isolation requirements, not convenience.

prompt: A BI tool uses a shared technical database user with broad SELECT access. What's the primary risk?

options.text: It bypasses individual accountability and often carries far more access than the reporting need requires
It's slightly slower than dedicated users
It requires more HANA Cockpit licenses
It only affects password rotation frequency

explanation: Shared broadly-privileged technical users are a major, frequently overlooked risk bypassing accountability entirely.

prompt: A contractor's access must be revoked urgently. Role collection was removed but access persists. What should the runbook mandate?

options.text: Disable the user at the identity provider (IAS) level to prevent new token issuance/refresh
Wait for the token to expire naturally

explanation: IdP-level disablement prevents new token issuance/refresh, addressing the true urgent-revocation gap role collection removal alone doesn't cover.

prompt: SSO goes down landscape-wide overnight across dev/QA/prod simultaneously. What's the most likely single cause?

options.text: A shared root/intermediate CA certificate, common across all three systems, expired

explanation: Simultaneous multi-system SSO outages point strongly to a shared certificate in the trust chain expiring, often a root/intermediate that wasn't individually monitored.

prompt: How should IPS-managed target ownership be decided to avoid manual fixes being silently reverted?

options.text: Explicitly document per target whether it's fully IPS-managed or hybrid, and enforce that boundary in change management
Let each administrator decide informally on a case-by-case basis
Disable IPS sync entirely for any target with manual changes

explanation: Undocumented ownership boundaries cause the classic 'manual fix keeps reverting' pattern.

prompt: Principal Propagation worked for 5 pilot users but fails at wider rollout to 500. What's the likely architecture flaw?

options.text: Manual 1:1 user mapping table instead of attribute-based identity federation

explanation: Manually maintained mapping tables don't scale past a handful of pilot users; attribute-based federation is the scalable pattern.
